aWeek 9 & 10
1. Convex Set 2. Convex Combination 3. Convex Hull 4. Convex Function 5. Properties of Convex Functions 6. Concave Function 7. Necessary vs Sufficient vs Necessary & Sufficient Conditions (Meaning) 8. Constrained Optimization 9. Dot Product 10. Geometric Perspective of Stationarity Condition 11. Method Of Lagrange Multipliers (Example) 12. Projected Gradient Descent 13. Stochastic Gradient Descent
1. Convex SetA set S⊆RdS⊆Rd is a convex set, if, for all x1,x2∈Sx1,x2∈S, and for all 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1](𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2∈S(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2∈S NOTE :: From here, assume 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1], wherever used. ExampleS=a(x,y)ax+y=1aS={(x,y)|x+y=1}
x2=(1,0)x2=(1,0)x1=(0,1)x1=(0,1)(0.5,0.5)(0.5,0.5)ExampleS=ax∈R2a‖x‖2⩽9aS={x∈R2|‖x‖2⩽9}
x1x1x2x2(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2 PROOF• Triangular Inequality :: ‖u+v‖⩽‖u‖+‖v‖‖u+v‖⩽‖u‖+‖v‖, where u,v∈Rdu,v∈Rd• ‖c×u‖=|c|×‖u‖‖c×u‖=|c|×‖u‖, where c∈Rc∈R and u∈Rdu∈Rd
‖(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2‖⩽‖(𝜆)x1‖+‖(1-𝜆)x2‖
⩽(||𝜆||)‖x1‖+(||1-𝜆||)‖x2‖
⩽(𝜆)3+(1-𝜆)3
⩽3
‖(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2‖2⩽9
‖(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2‖⩽‖(𝜆)x1‖+‖(1-𝜆)x2‖⩽(|𝜆|)‖x1‖+(|1-𝜆|)‖x2‖⩽(𝜆)3+(1-𝜆)3⩽3‖(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2‖2⩽9 Therefore, SS is a convex set.Non-ExampleThe following is not a convex set
x1x1x2x2(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2ExampleShow that S=ax∈RdawTx=baS={x∈Rd|wTx=b} is a convex set. • Let x1,x2∈Sx1,x2∈S, i.e., wTx1=bwTx1=b and wTx2=bwTx2=b• Let v=(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2v=(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2, 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1]
wTv=wT[(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2]
=(𝜆)wTx1+(1-𝜆)wTx2
=(𝜆)b+(1-𝜆)b
=b
wTv=wT[(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2]=(𝜆)wTx1+(1-𝜆)wTx2=(𝜆)b+(1-𝜆)b=bv∈Sv∈S. Therefore, SS is a convex set. ExampleShow that S=ax∈RdawTx⩽baS={x∈Rd|wTx⩽b} is a convex set. • Let x1,x2∈Sx1,x2∈S, i.e., wTx1<bwTx1<b and wTx2<bwTx2<b• Let v=(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2v=(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2, 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1] Notice, • (𝜆)wTx1⩽(𝜆)b(𝜆)wTx1⩽(𝜆)b• (1-𝜆)wTx2⩽(1-𝜆)b(1-𝜆)wTx2⩽(1-𝜆)b
wTv=wT[(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2]
=(𝜆)wTx1+(1-𝜆)wTx2
⩽(𝜆)b+(1-𝜆)b
⩽b
wTv=wT[(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2]=(𝜆)wTx1+(1-𝜆)wTx2⩽(𝜆)b+(1-𝜆)b⩽bv∈Sv∈S. Therefore, SS is a convex set. ExampleShow that S=ax∈Rda(An×d)x=baS={x∈Rd|(An×d)x=b} is a convex set. • Let x1,x2∈Sx1,x2∈S, i.e., (An×d)x1=b(An×d)x1=b and (An×d)x2=b(An×d)x2=b• Let v=(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2v=(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2, 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1]
(An×d)v=(An×d)[(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2]
=(𝜆)(An×d)x1+(1-𝜆)(An×d)x2
=(𝜆)b+(1-𝜆)b
=b
(An×d)v=(An×d)[(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2]=(𝜆)(An×d)x1+(1-𝜆)(An×d)x2=(𝜆)b+(1-𝜆)b=b v∈Sv∈S. Therefore, SS is a convex set. A Property Of Convex SetIntersection of convex sets is convex. PROOF• Let S1S1 and S2S2 be convex sets.• x1,x2∈S1x1,x2∈S1• x1,x2∈S2x1,x2∈S2• Let x1,x2∈S1∩S2x1,x2∈S1∩S2 Since S1S1 is a convex set, we can say that (𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2∈S1(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2∈S1, 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1]Since S2S2 is a convex set, we can say that (𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2∈S2(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2∈S2, 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1]Therefore, (𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2∈S1∩S2(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2∈S1∩S2. S1∩S2S1∩S2 is a convex set EXAMPLEShow that S=ax∈RdaAn×dx=baS={x∈Rd|An×dx=b} is a convex set. A=a
―a1―
―a2―
⋮
―an―
, x=a
x1
x2
⋮
xd
, b=a
b1
b2
⋮
bn
A=[―a1――a2―⋮―an―],x=[x1x2⋮xd],b=[b1b2⋮bn]
S=ax∈RdaAn×dx=ba
=ax∈RdaaT1x=b1,...,aTnx=bna
={x∈Rd|aT1x=b1}∩...∩{x∈Rd|aTnx=bn}
S={x∈Rd|An×dx=b}={x∈Rd|a1Tx=b1,...,anTx=bn}={x∈Rd|a1Tx=b1}∩...∩{x∈Rd|anTx=bn} {x∈Rd|aTix=bi}{x∈Rd|aiTx=bi} is a convex set. SS is an intersection of convex sets and therefore, SS is a convex set.
2. Convex CombinationLet S={x1,...,xn}∈RdS={x1,...,xn}∈Rd. z∈Rdz∈Rd is said to be a convex combination of x1,...,xnx1,...,xn if z=n∑i=1𝛼ixiz=∑i=1n𝛼ixiwhere 𝛼i⩾0, ∀i∈{1,...,n}𝛼i⩾0,∀i∈{1,...,n} andn∑i=1𝛼i=1∑i=1n𝛼i=1 Example : Let S={x1,...,x5}S={x1,...,x5}.
x1x1x3x3x2x2x4x4x5x5zz
3. Convex HullLet S={x1,...,xn}⊆RdS={x1,...,xn}⊆Rd. Then Convex-Hull(S)={z∈Rdaz=n∑i=1𝛼ixi, 𝛼1,...,𝛼n⩾0,n∑i=1𝛼i=1}Convex-Hull(S)={z∈Rd|z=∑i=1n𝛼ixi,𝛼1,...,𝛼n⩾0,∑i=1n𝛼i=1} In simple words, it is the set of all possible convex combinations of the elements in set SS. Convex-Hull(S)Convex-Hull(S) is a convex set. Proof is given below. • Let z1,z2∈Convex-Hull(S)z1,z2∈Convex-Hull(S)• z1=n∑i=1𝛼(1,i)xiz1=∑i=1n𝛼(1,i)xi where, a(1,i)>0, ∀i∈{1,...,n}a(1,i)>0,∀i∈{1,...,n} and n∑i=1𝛼1,i=1∑i=1n𝛼1,i=1• z2=n∑i=1𝛼(2,i)xiz2=∑i=1n𝛼(2,i)xi where, a(2,i)>0, ∀i∈{1,...,n}a(2,i)>0,∀i∈{1,...,n} and n∑i=1𝛼2,i=1∑i=1n𝛼2,i=1• Let z'
be
 (𝜆)z1+(1-𝜆)z2z'be(𝜆)z1+(1-𝜆)z2, 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1]
z'=(𝜆)z1+(1-𝜆)z2
=(𝜆)n∑i=1𝛼(1,i)xi+(1-𝜆)n∑i=1𝛼(2,i)xi
=n∑i=1(𝜆𝛼(1,i))xi+n∑i=1((1-𝜆)𝛼(2,i))xi
=n∑i=1a(𝜆𝛼1,i)+((1-𝜆)𝛼2,i)axi
z'=(𝜆)z1+(1-𝜆)z2=(𝜆)∑i=1n𝛼(1,i)xi+(1-𝜆)∑i=1n𝛼(2,i)xi=∑i=1n(𝜆𝛼(1,i))xi+∑i=1n((1-𝜆)𝛼(2,i))xi=∑i=1n[(𝜆𝛼1,i)+((1-𝜆)𝛼2,i)]xi Notice, (𝜆𝛼(1,i))+((1-𝜆)𝛼(2,i))⩾0(𝜆𝛼(1,i))+((1-𝜆)𝛼(2,i))⩾0 ∀i∈{1,...,n}∀i∈{1,...,n}.
n∑i=1a(𝜆𝛼(1,i))+((1-𝜆)𝛼(2,i))a=𝜆n∑i=1𝛼(1,i)+(1-𝜆)n∑i=1𝛼(2,i)
=𝜆(1)+(1-𝜆)(1)
=1
∑i=1n[(𝜆𝛼(1,i))+((1-𝜆)𝛼(2,i))]=𝜆∑i=1n𝛼(1,i)+(1-𝜆)∑i=1n𝛼(2,i)=𝜆(1)+(1-𝜆)(1)=1 z'z' is a convex combination of the elements in SS. Therefore, Convex-Hull(S)Convex-Hull(S) is a convex set. ExampleLet S={x1,...,x5}S={x1,...,x5}
x1x1x3x3x2x2x4x4x5x5zzIn the above picture, zz is a convex combination of x1,...,x5x1,...,x5 and the brown shaded region is the convex hull of SS. ALTERNATE DEFINITIONConvex-Hull({x1,...,xn})Convex-Hull({x1,...,xn}) is the intersection of all the convex sets containing {x1,...,xn}{x1,...,xn}.
4. Convex FunctionDEFINITION 1Let D⊆RdD⊆Rd be a convex set. A function f:D→Rf:D→R is a convex function if epi(f)epi(f) is a convex set. epi(f)={a
x
z
∈Rd+1az⩾f(x)}epi(f)={[xz]∈Rd+1|z⩾f(x)} Here, a
x
z
=a
x1x2⋯xdz
T[xz]=[x1x2⋯xdz]T.
CONVEX SETNOT A CONVEX SETxxxxf(x)f(x)f(x)f(x) DEFINITION 2Let D⊆RdD⊆Rd be a convex set. A function f:D→Rf:D→R is a convex function if and only if ∀x1,x2∈Rd∀x1,x2∈Rd and for all 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1],f((𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2)⩽𝜆f(x1)+(1-𝜆)f(x2)f((𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2)⩽𝜆f(x1)+(1-𝜆)f(x2)
f((𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2)f((𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2)xxf(x)f(x)x1x1x2x2(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2f(x1)f(x1)f(x2)f(x2)𝜆f(x1)+(1-𝜆)f(x2)𝜆f(x1)+(1-𝜆)f(x2)CONVEXFUNCTION
f((𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2)f((𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2)xxf(x)f(x)x1x1x2x2(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2f(x1)f(x1)f(x2)f(x2)𝜆f(x1)+(1-𝜆)f(x2)𝜆f(x1)+(1-𝜆)f(x2)NOTACONVEXFUNCTION DEFINITION 3Let D⊆RdD⊆Rd be a convex set. A differentiable function f:D→Rf:D→R is a convex function if and only iff(x)⩾f(a)+(x-a)T▽f(a)f(x)⩾f(a)+(x-a)T▽f(a)for all a∈Da∈D In simple terms, this means that the value of the function at any point is always greater than or equal to its linear approximation at that point.
f(x)=x2-2x+2f(x)=x2-2x+2L(x)=1.25+(x-1.5)(1)L(x)=1.25+(x-1.5)(1)a
f(1.5)=1.25
▽f(1.5)=1
f(1.5)=1.25▽f(1.5)=1(1.5,1.25)(1.5,1.25)f(x)f(x)L(x)L(x)CONVEXFUNCTION
f(x)=x3-2x2-2x+2f(x)=x3-2x2-2x+2L(x)=2+(x-0)(-2)L(x)=2+(x-0)(-2)a
f(0)=2
▽f(0)=-2
f(0)=2▽f(0)=-2(0,2)(0,2)f(x)f(x)L(x)L(x)NOT ACONVEXFUNCTION DEFINITION 4Let D⊆RdD⊆Rd be a convex set. Let a function f:D→Rf:D→R be twice differentiable. HH is a d×dd×d matrix such that, for all i,j∈{1,2,...,d}i,j∈{1,2,...,d}Hij=𝛿f2
𝛿xi𝛿xjHij=𝛿f2𝛿xi𝛿xj ff is a convex function if and only if Hf(v)Hf(v) is a positive semi-definite matrix for all v∈Dv∈D. If ff is a function of one variable, i.e.,i.e., if D⊆RD⊆R, then ff is convex iff''(x)⩾0, ∀x∈Df''(x)⩾0,∀x∈D
5. Properties of Convex FunctionsPROPERTY 1The domain of a convex function is always a convex set. PROOFLet f:D→Rf:D→R be a convex function. Let us assume that DD is not a convex set. Since DD is not a convex function, for some x,y∈Dx,y∈D, where x≠yx≠y, there exists a 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1] such that 𝜆x+(1-𝜆)y∉D𝜆x+(1-𝜆)y∉D Since ff is a convex function, we can say:f(𝜆x+(1-𝜆)y)⩽𝜆f(x)+(1-𝜆)f(y)f(𝜆x+(1-𝜆)y)⩽𝜆f(x)+(1-𝜆)f(y) Since ff is convex, the above inequality must hold true. But, 𝜆x+(1-𝜆)y𝜆x+(1-𝜆)y is not in DD. ff cannot be defined at 𝜆x+(1-𝜆)y𝜆x+(1-𝜆)y. There is a contradiction. Our assumption is incorrect. Therefore, ff is not a convex function. PROPERTY 2Let D∈RdD∈Rd be a convex set. If f:D→Rf:D→R is a convex function, then all local minima of ff are also global minima. PROOFAssume the following • xx is a local minimum of ff but not a global minimum of ff• zz is a global minimum of ff• f(z)<f(x)f(z)<f(x) Since xx is a local minimum, we can say that there exist a 𝛿>0𝛿>0 such that f(x)⩽f(v)f(x)⩽f(v) for all vv in the following set.S={va||x-v||⩽𝛿}S={v|||x-v||⩽𝛿} The above set is represented by the following circle. Let us take a small step from xx towards zz such that we stay within set SS. It is given by 𝜆x+(1-𝜆)z,𝜆x+(1-𝜆)z, 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1].
Set SSxxzz𝜆x+(1-𝜆)z𝜆x+(1-𝜆)z From our assumption, f(z)<f(x)f(z)<f(x). Therefore,𝜆f(x)+(1-𝜆)f(z)<𝜆f(x)+(1-𝜆)f(x)𝜆f(x)+(1-𝜆)f(z)<𝜆f(x)+(1-𝜆)f(x) 𝜆x+(1-𝜆)z∈S𝜆x+(1-𝜆)z∈S. Therefore,
f(x)⩽f(𝜆x+(1-𝜆)z)
f(x)⩽𝜆f(x)+(1-𝜆)f(z)
f(x)<𝜆f(x)+(1-𝜆)f(x)
f(x)<f(x)
f(x)⩽f(𝜆x+(1-𝜆)z)f(x)⩽𝜆f(x)+(1-𝜆)f(z)f(x)<𝜆f(x)+(1-𝜆)f(x)f(x)<f(x)a
f(x)<f(x)
f(x)<f(x)is a contradiction. Therefore, our assumption that f(z)<f(x)f(z)<f(x) is incorrect. Therefore, xx is also a global minimum of ff. PROPERTY 3Let D∈RdD∈Rd be a convex set and let f:D→Rf:D→R be a differentiable convex function. z∈Dz∈D is a global minimum if and only if ∇f(z)=0∇f(z)=0. PROOFTo prove that the above property is true, we must show that the following are true.• If zz is a global minimum, then ∇f(z)=0∇f(z)=0• If ∇f(z)=0∇f(z)=0, then ff is a global minimum (A)(A) If zz is a global minimum, then ∇f(z)=0∇f(z)=0 Let us assume that zz is a global minimum of ff and ∇f(z)≠0∇f(z)≠0. Since ∇f(z)≠0∇f(z)≠0, -∇f(z)-∇f(z) is the direction of steepest descent at zz. Since zz is a global minimum, zz is also a local minimum. There exists a 𝛿>0𝛿>0 such that f(z)⩽f(y)f(z)⩽f(y) for all y∈Sy∈S, whereS={ya||z-y||⩽𝛿}S={y|||z-y||⩽𝛿} From zz, let us move to qq, whereq=z-𝛽∇f(z)
||∇f(z)||q=z-𝛽∇f(z)||∇f(z)||and 0<𝛽<𝛿0<𝛽<𝛿.
Set SSzzqq-∇f(z)-∇f(z)Since -∇f(z)-∇f(z) is the direction of steepest descent,f(q)<f(z)f(q)<f(z) Since 0<𝛽<𝛿0<𝛽<𝛿, q∈Sq∈S. Since q∈zq∈z, f(q)⩾f(z)f(q)⩾f(z). We have a contradiction. Our assumption is incorrect. Therefore, ∇f(z)=0∇f(z)=0 (B)(B) If ∇f(z)=0∇f(z)=0, then ff is a global minimum From the definition of convex function, for all y∈Dy∈D,f(y)⩾f(z)+∇f(z)T(y-z)f(y)⩾f(z)+∇f(z)T(y-z) Since ∇f(z)=0∇f(z)=0, we getf(y)⩾f(z)f(y)⩾f(z)Therefore, zz is a global minimum. PROPERTY 4The sum of two convex functions is a convex function. PROOFLet D∈RdD∈Rd be a convex set and let f:D→Rf:D→R and g:D→Rg:D→R be convex functions. We have to show that h:D→Rh:D→R is a convex function, whereh(x)=f(x)+g(x), ∀x∈Dh(x)=f(x)+g(x),∀x∈D Let u,v∈Rdu,v∈Rd and let 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1]
h(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v)=f(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v)+g(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v)
⩽𝜆f(u)+(1-𝜆)f(v)+𝜆g(u)+(1-𝜆)g(v)
⩽𝜆[f(u)+g(u)]+(1-𝜆)[f(v)+g(v)]
⩽𝜆h(u)+(1-𝜆)h(v)
h(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v)=f(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v)+g(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v)⩽𝜆f(u)+(1-𝜆)f(v)+𝜆g(u)+(1-𝜆)g(v)⩽𝜆[f(u)+g(u)]+(1-𝜆)[f(v)+g(v)]⩽𝜆h(u)+(1-𝜆)h(v) Therefore, h(x)h(x) is a convex function. PROPERTY 5Let DD be a convex set. Let f:R→Rf:R→R be a non-decreasing convex function and let g:D→Rg:D→R be a convex function. Then, hh is a convex function, whereh(x)=f(g(x)), ∀x∈Dh(x)=f(g(x)),∀x∈D PROOFSince ff is a non decreasing function, we can say that for all x,y∈Rx,y∈R, if x⩽yx⩽y, thenf(x)⩽f(y)f(x)⩽f(y) Let u,v∈Du,v∈D and let 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1]a
g(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v)⩽𝜆g(u)+(1-𝜆)g(v)
g(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v)⩽𝜆g(u)+(1-𝜆)g(v) Thereofore,
h(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v)=f(g(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v)
⩽f(𝜆g(u)+(1-𝜆)g(v))
⩽𝜆f(g(u))+(1-𝜆)f(g(v))
⩽𝜆h(u)+(1-𝜆)h(v)
h(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v)=f(g(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v)⩽f(𝜆g(u)+(1-𝜆)g(v))⩽𝜆f(g(u))+(1-𝜆)f(g(v))⩽𝜆h(u)+(1-𝜆)h(v)Therefore, hh is a convex function PROPERTY 6Let DD be a convex set. Let f:R→Rf:R→R be a convex function and let g:D→Rg:D→R be a linear function. Then, hh is a convex function, whereh(x)=f(g(x)), ∀x∈Dh(x)=f(g(x)),∀x∈D PROOFSince gg is a linear function, the following is true for all u,v∈Du,v∈D and for all 𝛼,𝛽∈R𝛼,𝛽∈Rg(𝛼u+𝛽v)=𝛼g(u)+𝛽g(v)g(𝛼u+𝛽v)=𝛼g(u)+𝛽g(v) Let 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1]
h(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v)=f(g(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v))
=f(𝜆g(u)+(1-𝜆)g(v))
⩽𝜆f(g(u))+(1-𝜆)f(g(v))
⩽𝜆h(u)+(1-𝜆)h(v)
h(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v)=f(g(𝜆u+(1-𝜆)v))=f(𝜆g(u)+(1-𝜆)g(v))⩽𝜆f(g(u))+(1-𝜆)f(g(v))⩽𝜆h(u)+(1-𝜆)h(v)Therefore, hh is a convex function CaveatIn general, the composition of two convex functions need not be convex. Here is an example.f(x)=e-xg(x)=x2f(x)=e-xg(x)=x2 h(x)=e-x2h(x)=e-x2 is not a convex function.
h(x)=e-x2h(x)=e-x2
6. Concave FunctionA function f(x)f(x) is concave if -f(x)-f(x) is convex.
x2x2-x2-x2 The definitions and properties of concave functions are similar to that of convex functions. Therefore, we will list them without proofs. DEFINITION 1A function f:D→Rf:D→R is a concave function if the hypograph of ff, denoted by hyp(f)hyp(f) is a convex set.hyp(f)={a
x
z
∈Rd+1az⩽f(x)}hyp(f)={[xz]∈Rd+1|z⩽f(x)} Here, a
x
z
=a
x1x2⋯xdz
T[xz]=[x1x2⋯xdz]T.
CONVEX SETNOT A CONVEX SETxxxxf(x)f(x)f(x)f(x) DEFINITION 2A function f:D→Rf:D→R is a concave function if and only if ∀x1,x2∈Rd∀x1,x2∈Rd and for all 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1],f((𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2)⩾𝜆f(x1)+(1-𝜆)f(x2)f((𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2)⩾𝜆f(x1)+(1-𝜆)f(x2)
f((𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2)f((𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2)xxf(x)f(x)x1x1x2x2(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2f(x1)f(x1)f(x2)f(x2)𝜆f(x1)+(1-𝜆)f(x2)𝜆f(x1)+(1-𝜆)f(x2)CONCAVEFUNCTION
f((𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2)f((𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2)xxf(x)f(x)x1x1x2x2(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2(𝜆)x1+(1-𝜆)x2f(x1)f(x1)f(x2)f(x2)𝜆f(x1)+(1-𝜆)f(x2)𝜆f(x1)+(1-𝜆)f(x2)NOTACONCAVEFUNCTION DEFINITION 3A differentiable function f:D→Rf:D→R is a concave function if and only iff(x)⩽f(a)+(x-a)T▽f(a)f(x)⩽f(a)+(x-a)T▽f(a)for all a∈Da∈D In simple terms, this means that the value of the function at any point is always less than or equal to its linear approximation at that point.
f(x)=-x2+2x-2f(x)=-x2+2x-2L(x)=-1.25-(1)(x-1.5)L(x)=-1.25-(1)(x-1.5)a
f(1.5)=1.25
▽f(1.5)=1
f(1.5)=1.25▽f(1.5)=1(1.5,-1.25)(1.5,-1.25)f(x)f(x)L(x)L(x)CONCAVEFUNCTION
f(x)=x3-2x2-2x+2f(x)=x3-2x2-2x+2L(x)=-2.125-1.25(x-1.5)L(x)=-2.125-1.25(x-1.5)a
f(1.5)=-2.125
▽f(1.25)=-1.25
f(1.5)=-2.125▽f(1.25)=-1.25(1.5,-2.125)(1.5,-2.125)f(x)f(x)L(x)L(x)NOT ACONCAVEFUNCTION DEFINITION 4Let a function f:D→Rf:D→R be twice differentiable. HH is a d×dd×d matrix such that for all i,j∈{1,2,...,d}i,j∈{1,2,...,d}Hij=𝛿f2
𝛿xi𝛿xjHij=𝛿f2𝛿xi𝛿xj ff is a concave function if and only if Hf(v)Hf(v) is a negative semi-definite matrix for all v∈Dv∈D. If ff is a function of one variable, i.e.,i.e., if D∈RD∈R, then ff is concave iff''(x)⩽0, ∀x∈Df''(x)⩽0,∀x∈D PROPERTIES• The domain of a concave function is always a convex set.• Let ff be a concave function. All the local maxima of ff are also the global maxima of ff.• Let ff be a differentiable concave function. zz is a global maximum of ff if and only if ∇f(z)=0∇f(z)=0.• The sum of two concave functions is concave.• Let D∈RdD∈Rd be a convex set. Let f:R→Rf:R→R be a non-decreasing concave function and let g:D→Rg:D→R be a concave function. Then, h(x)=f(g(x)), ∀x∈Dh(x)=f(g(x)),∀x∈D is a concave function.• Let D∈RdD∈Rd be a convex set. Let f:R→Rf:R→R be a concave function and let g:D→Rg:D→R be a linear function. Then, h(x)=f(g(x)), ∀x∈Dh(x)=f(g(x)),∀x∈D is a concave function.• Caveat : The composition of two concave function, need not be concave, Let f(x)=-exf(x)=-ex and let g(x)-x2g(x)-x2. ff and gg are concave. However, h(x)=f(g(x))=-e-x2h(x)=f(g(x))=-e-x2 is not concave
7. Necessary vs Sufficient vs Necessary & Sufficient Conditions (Meaning)Through an example let us understand the meaning of necessary condition and sufficient condition and necessary and sufficient condition Statement : A number nn is divisible by 66• Necessary Condition : nn is divisible by 22• Sufficient Condition : nn is divisible by 1212• Necessary & Sufficient Conditon : nn is divisible by 22 and 33 • A necessary condition is something that must be true, but by itself does not guarantee the result. • A sufficient condition is something that guarantees the result. but is not required for the statement to be always true.• A necessary and sufficent condition is something that guarantees the result and is required for the statement to be true.
8. Constrained OptimizationGeneral Form
min
xf(x)s.t.p1(x)⩽0,...,pn(x)⩽0q1(x)=0,...,qm(x)=0minxf(x)s.t.p1(x)⩽0,...,pn(x)⩽0q1(x)=0,...,qm(x)=0 • f(x)f(x) is the objective function• p1(x)⩽0,...,pn(x)⩽0p1(x)⩽0,...,pn(x)⩽0 are the inequality constraints• q1(x)=0,...,qm(x)=0q1(x)=0,...,qm(x)=0 are the equality constraints Lagrangian FunctionFor the general form of constrained optimization, the following function is called the lagrangian function.L(x,𝛼,𝛽)=f(x)+n∑i=1𝛼ipi(x)+m∑j=1𝛽jqj(x)L(x,𝛼,𝛽)=f(x)+∑i=1n𝛼ipi(x)+∑j=1m𝛽jqj(x)where,𝛼=a
𝛼1
𝛼2
⋮
𝛼n
, 𝛽=a
𝛽1
𝛽2
⋮
𝛽m
𝛼=(𝛼1𝛼2⋮𝛼n),𝛽=(𝛽1𝛽2⋮𝛽m) 𝛼1,...,𝛼n𝛼1,...,𝛼n and 𝛽1,...,𝛽m𝛽1,...,𝛽m are called the lagrange multipliers. The multiplers of inequalty constraints must be non-negative. That is, 𝛼i⩾0, ∀i∈{1,...,n}𝛼i⩾0,∀i∈{1,...,n}. Primal FormulationLet us focus on
min
xf(x)s.t.g(x)⩽0minxf(x)s.t.g(x)⩽0 The above formulation is referred to as the primal formulation. Consider the Lagrangian function, (𝜆⩾0)(𝜆⩾0)L(x,𝜆)=f(x)+𝜆g(x)L(x,𝜆)=f(x)+𝜆g(x) For a fixed xx^ in the domain of ff, let us consider the following.
max
𝜆⩾0f(x)+𝜆g(x)max𝜆⩾0f(x^)+𝜆g(x^) • If h(x)⩽0h(x^)⩽0, then f(x)+𝜆g(x)f(x^)+𝜆g(x^) is maximum when 𝜆=0𝜆=0.• If h(x)>0h(x^)>0, then
max
𝜆⩾0f(x)+𝜆g(x)=0max𝜆⩾0f(x^)+𝜆g(x^)=0. As we increase 𝜆𝜆, 𝜆g(x)𝜆g(x^) increases. Since we are maximizing with respect to 𝜆⩾0𝜆⩾0, we can arbitrarily increase 𝜆𝜆. In the figure, given below, let the yellow region be the constraint set SS.S={x|g(x)⩽0}S={x|g(x)⩽0} Outside the yellow region, the constraint g(x)⩽0g(x)⩽0 is not met.
g(x)⩽0g(x)⩽0g(x)>0g(x)>0x1x1x2x2x3x3x4x4x5x5x1,x2x1,x2 and x3x3 are within the constraint set and x4x4 and x5x5 are outside the constraint set. Let us fix these points and maximize the Lagrangian function with respect to 𝜆⩾0𝜆⩾0 Fix x1x1
max
𝜆⩾0f(x1)+𝜆g(x1)=f(x1)max𝜆⩾0f(x1)+𝜆g(x1)=f(x1)Fix x2x2
max

max
𝜆⩾0f(x3)+𝜆g(x3)=f(x3)max𝜆⩾0f(x3)+𝜆g(x3)=f(x3)Fix x4x4

𝜆⩾0f(x5)+𝜆g(x5)=∞max𝜆⩾0f(x5)+𝜆g(x5)=∞ Suppose, the domain of ff is {x1,x2,x3,x4,x5}{x1,x2,x3,x4,x5}, thena

xf(x)s.tg(x)⩽0
=minaf(x1),f(x2),f(x3)aminxf(x)s.tg(x)⩽0=min(f(x1),f(x2),f(x3)) This is true as g(x)⩽0g(x)⩽0 is satisfied only at x1,x2x1,x2 and x3x3. Similarly,

max

xaf(x)+𝜆g(x)amax𝜆⩾0minx[f(x)+𝜆g(x)] In the dual formulation, we have interchanged minmin and maxmax. This leads us to two concepts.• Weak Duality• Strong Duality Weak DualityConsider the following function.
J(x)=aa
f(x),if g(x)⩽0
∞,if g(x)>0
J(x)={f(x),ifg(x)⩽0∞,ifg(x)>0For any fixed 𝜆⩾0𝜆⩾0• If g(x)⩽0g(x)⩽0, then 𝜆g(x)⩽0𝜆g(x)⩽0. Therefore,L(x,𝜆)=f(x)+𝜆g(x)⩽f(x)L(x,𝜆)=f(x)+𝜆g(x)⩽f(x)• If g(x)⩽0g(x)⩽0, then 𝜆g(x)⩾0𝜆g(x)⩾0. Therefore,L(x,𝜆)=f(x)+𝜆g(x)⩽∞L(x,𝜆)=f(x)+𝜆g(x)⩽∞ Therefore, for any fixed 𝜆⩾0𝜆⩾0, and for all xx in the domain of ff,
L(x,𝜆)=f(x)+𝜆g(x)⩽J(x)
L(x,𝜆)=f(x)+𝜆g(x)⩽J(x) Let x*x* be the solution for the primal formulation. That is,x*=
argmin
xf(x), s.t., g(x)⩽0x*=argminxf(x),s.t.,g(x)⩽0Note that x*x* satisfies g(x*)⩽0g(x*)⩽0. Therefore,x*=

xJ(x)=f(x*)minxJ(x)=f(x*) Equation (1)(1) is true for any fixed 𝜆⩾0𝜆⩾0 for all xx in the domain of ff. Therefore,a
min
xaf(x)+𝜆g(x)a		⩽

xaf(x)+𝜆g(x)a		⩽f(x*)
minx[f(x)+𝜆g(x)]⩽minxJ(x)minx[f(x)+𝜆g(x)]⩽f(x*) The above inequality is true for any 𝜆⩾0𝜆⩾0. Therefore,a
max
𝜆⩾0
min
xaf(x)+𝜆g(x)a		⩽f(x*)
max𝜆⩾0minx[f(x)+𝜆g(x)]⩽f(x*) The left hand side of the inequality is the dual formulation. The right hand side is the value of f(x)f(x) at the primal optimal x*x*. The dual formulation provides a lower bound for the optimal value of primal formulation. This property is called the weak duality. Strong DualityIf f(x)f(x) and g(x)g(x) are convex, and they satisfy some regularity conditions (for our purpose, we can ignore those), then the following is true.
max
𝜆⩾0
min
xaf(x)+𝜆g(x)a=f(x*)max𝜆⩾0minx[f(x)+𝜆g(x)]=f(x*) This property is referred to as the strong duality. When strong duality holds, we can either solve the primal problem or the dual problem. KKT Condition (Informal)Let us define x*x* and 𝜆*𝜆* as follows,x*=
argmin
xf(x), s.t., g(x)⩽0 𝜆*=
argmax
𝜆⩾0
min
xaf(x)+𝜆g(x)ax*=argminxf(x),s.t.,g(x)⩽0𝜆*=argmax𝜆⩾0minx[f(x)+𝜆g(x)] x*x* is the primal optimal and 𝜆*𝜆* is the dual optimal. Suppose, strong duality holds, then what are the necessary conditions that x*x* and 𝜆*𝜆* satisfy?? Stationarity Condition and Complementary Slackeness ConditionLet us focus on the Lagrangian function at x*x* and 𝜆*𝜆*L(x*,𝜆*)=f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)L(x*,𝜆*)=f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*) 𝜆*g(x*)⩽0𝜆*g(x*)⩽0. Therefore,
L(x*,𝜆*)⩽f(x*)
f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)⩽f(x*)
L(x*,𝜆*)⩽f(x*)f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)⩽f(x*) Let us now focus the dual formulation. Since 𝜆*𝜆* is the dual optimal,
max
𝜆⩾0
min
xaf(x)+𝜆g(x)a=
min
xaf(x)+𝜆*g(x)amax𝜆⩾0minx[f(x)+𝜆g(x)]=minx[f(x)+𝜆*g(x)] We are minimizing L(x,𝜆*)=f(x)+𝜆*g(x)L(x,𝜆*)=f(x)+𝜆*g(x) with respect to xx. Suppose x'x' is the argument that minimizes L(x,𝜆*)L(x,𝜆*)x'=
argmin
xf(x)+𝜆*g(x)x'=argminxf(x)+𝜆*g(x) then, L(x',𝜆*)⩽L(x,𝜆*)L(x',𝜆*)⩽L(x,𝜆*) for all xx in the domain of ff. Therefore, it should be true for x*x*,
f(x')+𝜆*g(x')⩽f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)
f(x')+𝜆*g(x')⩽f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*) From inequalities (A)(A) and (B)(B), we get the following.f(x')+𝜆*g(x')⩽f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)⩽f(x*)f(x')+𝜆*g(x')⩽f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)⩽f(x*) We know thatf(x')+𝜆*g(x')=
min
xf(x)+𝜆*g(x)f(x')+𝜆*g(x')=minxf(x)+𝜆*g(x) Therefore,
min
xf(x)+𝜆*g(x)⩽f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)⩽f(x*)minxf(x)+𝜆*g(x)⩽f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)⩽f(x*) From strong duality, we know that
min
xf(x)+𝜆*g(x)=f(x*)minxf(x)+𝜆*g(x)=f(x*) Therefore,
min
xf(x)+𝜆*g(x)=f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)=f(x*)f(x')+𝜆*g(x')=f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)=f(x*)minxf(x)+𝜆*g(x)=f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)=f(x*)f(x')+𝜆*g(x')=f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)=f(x*) First, let us focus on f(x')+𝜆*g(x')=f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)f(x')+𝜆*g(x')=f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*). Recallx'=
argmin
xf(x)+𝜆*g(x)x'=argminxf(x)+𝜆*g(x)x'x' is a solution to an unconstrained optimization problem. Therefore, ∇f(x')+𝜆*∇g(x')=0∇f(x')+𝜆*∇g(x')=0.
f(x')+𝜆*g(x')=f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)
∇f(x')+𝜆*∇g(x')=∇f(x*)+𝜆*∇g(x*)
0=∇f(x*)+𝜆*∇g(x*)
f(x')+𝜆*g(x')=f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)∇f(x')+𝜆*∇g(x')=∇f(x*)+𝜆*∇g(x*)0=∇f(x*)+𝜆*∇g(x*) ∇f(x*)+𝜆*∇g(x*)=0∇f(x*)+𝜆*∇g(x*)=0 is called the stationarity condition. The gradient of the lagrangian at x*x* and 𝜆*𝜆* should be equal to 00. Let us focus on f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)=f(x*)f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)=f(x*)
f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)=f(x*)
𝜆*g(x*)=0
f(x*)+𝜆*g(x*)=f(x*)𝜆*g(x*)=0This is called the complementary slackness condition. Either g(x*)=0g(x*)=0 or 𝜆*=0𝜆*=0. KKT Condition (Formal)
min
xf(x)s.t.p1(x)⩽0,...,pn(x)⩽0q1(x)=0,...,qm(x)=0minxf(x)s.t.p1(x)⩽0,...,pn(x)⩽0q1(x)=0,...,qm(x)=0 The following is the lagrangian function for the above formulation.L(x,𝛼,𝛽)=f(x)+n∑i=1𝛼ipi(x)+m∑j=1𝛽jqj(x)L(x,𝛼,𝛽)=f(x)+∑i=1n𝛼ipi(x)+∑j=1m𝛽jqj(x)where,𝛼=a
𝛼1
𝛼2
⋮
𝛼n
, 𝛽=a
𝛽1
𝛽2
⋮
𝛽m
𝛼=(𝛼1𝛼2⋮𝛼n),𝛽=(𝛽1𝛽2⋮𝛽m) Let us define x*x*, 𝛼*𝛼* and 𝛽*𝛽* as followsx*=
argmin
x(
max
𝛼⩾0, 𝛽L(x,𝛼,𝛽)) 𝛼*,𝛽*=
argmax
𝛼⩾0, 𝛽(
min
xL(x,𝛼,𝛽))x*=argminx(max𝛼⩾0,𝛽L(x,𝛼,𝛽))𝛼*,𝛽*=argmax𝛼⩾0,𝛽(minxL(x,𝛼,𝛽)) Suppose, strong duality holds true. Then, the following are necessary condition that x*,x*,𝛼*𝛼* and 𝛽*𝛽* have to satisfy. They are called the KKT conditions. (A)(A) Stationarity Condition∇f(x*)+n∑i=1(𝛼*)∇pi(x*)+m∑j=1(𝛽*j)∇qj(x*)=0∇f(x*)+∑i=1n(𝛼*)∇pi(x*)+∑j=1m(𝛽j*)∇qj(x*)=0 (B)(B) Complementary Slackness Condition𝛼*ipi(x*)=0, ∀i∈{1,...,n}𝛼i*pi(x*)=0,∀i∈{1,...,n} Since qj(x*)=0qj(x*)=0, is true ∀j∈{1,...,m}∀j∈{1,...,m}, the condition 𝛽iqj(x*)=0𝛽iqj(x*)=0 automatically holds true ∀𝛽j∈R∀𝛽j∈R. Therefore, it does not impose any additional constraint and is not included as a KKT condition. (C)(C) Primal Feasibilitypi(x*)⩽0, ∀i∈{1,...,n}qj(x*)=0, ∀j∈{1,...,m}pi(x*)⩽0,∀i∈{1,...,n}qj(x*)=0,∀j∈{1,...,m} (D)(D) Dual Feasibility𝛼i⩾0, ∀i∈{1,...,n}𝛼i⩾0,∀i∈{1,...,n} 𝛽j𝛽j can be any real number, ∀j∈{1,...,m}∀j∈{1,...,m}. Sufficient ConditionSuppose, strong duality holds true. If x*,𝛼*x*,𝛼* and 𝛽*𝛽* satisfy the KKT conditions,• x*x* is the primal optimum• 𝛼*𝛼* and 𝛽*𝛽* are the dual optimal
9. Dot Product
wwwTx=0wTx=0wTx=𝛿wTx=𝛿𝛿>0𝛿>0wTx=-𝛿wTx=-𝛿w2w2w1w1wTw1>0wTw1>0wTw2<0wTw2<0• The angle between ww and w1w1 is less than 90°90°. Therefore, wTw1>0wTw1>0.• The angle between ww and w2w2 is greater than than 90°90°. Therefore, wTw2<0wTw2<0.
10. Geometric Perspective of Stationarity ConditionFor this section, let us assume that strong duality holds.Inequality Constraint
min
xf(x)s.t.g(x)⩽0minxf(x)s.t.g(x)⩽0 Let x*x* be the primal optimum and let 𝜆*𝜆* be the dual optimum. By stationarity condition,
∇f(x*)+𝜆*∇g(x*)=0
∇f(x*)=-𝜆*∇g(x*)
∇f(x*)+𝜆*∇g(x*)=0∇f(x*)=-𝜆*∇g(x*) Since 𝜆*⩾0,𝜆*⩾0, the gradient of ff at x*x* and the gradient of gg at x*x* are anti-parallel to each other.
▽f(x*)▽f(x*)▽f(x*)Td<0▽f(x*)Td<0▽g(x*)Tz<0▽g(x*)Tz<0▽g(x*)▽g(x*)▽f(x*)=-(𝜆*)▽g(x*)▽f(x*)=-(𝜆*)▽g(x*)From Taylor series,f(x*+d)≈f(x*)+∇f(x*)T(d)f(x*+d)-f(x*)≈∇f(x*)T(d)f(x*+d)≈f(x*)+∇f(x*)T(d)f(x*+d)-f(x*)≈∇f(x*)T(d)andg(x*+z)≈g(x*)+∇g(x*)T(z)g(x*+z)-g(x*)≈∇g(x*)T(z)g(x*+z)≈g(x*)+∇g(x*)T(z)g(x*+z)-g(x*)≈∇g(x*)T(z) The blue region is denoted by{da∇f(x*)Td⩽0}{d|∇f(x*)Td⩽0}Therefore,f(x*+d)-f(x*)⩽0f(x*+d)-f(x*)⩽0 Yellow region is denoted by{za∇g(x*)Tz⩽0}{z|∇g(x*)Tz⩽0}Therefore,g(x*+z)-g(x*)⩽0g(x*+z)-g(x*)⩽0 • dd is a descent direction of ff at x*x* • zz is a descent direction of gg at x*x*. Descent Direction : For a function h(x)h(x), dd is a descent direction at x*x*, if for some sufficiently small 𝛽>0𝛽>0, h(x+𝛽d)<h(x)h(x+𝛽d)<h(x). Equality Constraint
min
xf(x)s.t.g(x)=0minxf(x)s.t.g(x)=0 Let x*x* be the primal optimum and let 𝜆*𝜆* be the dual optimum. By stationarity condition,
∇f(x*)+𝜆*∇g(x*)=0
∇f(x*)=-𝜆*∇g(x*)
∇f(x*)+𝜆*∇g(x*)=0∇f(x*)=-𝜆*∇g(x*)For equality constrian, 𝜆𝜆 can be any real number. Therefore, ∇f(x*)∇f(x*) and ∇g(x*)∇g(x*) are scalar multiples of each other.
▽f(x*)▽f(x*)dT▽f(x*)<0dT▽f(x*)<0▽g(x*)▽g(x*)dT▽g(x*)=0dT▽g(x*)=0▽f(x*)▽f(x*)dT▽f(x*)<0dT▽f(x*)<0▽g(x*)▽g(x*)dT▽g(x*)=0dT▽g(x*)=0 The pink line represents all the directions dd such that::dT∇g(x*)=0dT∇g(x*)=0Along these directions, the value of the function g(x)g(x) remains equal to g(x*)g(x*). Since x*x* is the primal optimal, it satisfies g(x*)=0g(x*)=0. As a result, the value of the function g(x)g(x) along the pink line is also 00. For sufficiently small steps along the pink line, the constraint g(x)=0g(x)=0 remains satisfied. The blue region corresponds to {dadT∇f(x*)⩽0}{d|dT∇f(x*)⩽0}. The blue region are the descent directions of ff at x*x*. Notice that the blue region and the pink line do not intesect. If they were to intersect, then there would exist a direction along the pink line that is also a descent direction for ff at x*x*. In that case, starting from x*x* we could take a sufficiently small step along the pink direction and reduce the zvalue of the objective function f(x)f(x) while still satisfying the constraint g(x)=0g(x)=0.. This would imply that x*x* is not an optimal solution.
11. Method Of Lagrange Multipliers (Example)minx1,x2 [f(x1,x2)=x21+2x2+4x22] s.t g(x1,x2)= 0 g(x1,x2)=x21 +x22-1minx1,x2[f(x1,x2)=x12+2x2+4x22]s.tg(x1,x2)=0g(x1,x2)=x12+x22-1 Find gradient
▽f(x1,x2)=(2x1,2+8x2)
▽g(x1,x2)=(2x1,2x2)
▽f(x1,x2)=(2x1,2+8x2)▽g(x1,x2)=(2x1,2x2) We have an equaity constraint. By stationarity condition, (𝜆∈R)(𝜆∈R)
▽f(x1,x2)=-𝜆×▽g(x1,x2)
(2x1,2+8x2)=-𝜆(2x1,2x2)
▽f(x1,x2)=-𝜆×▽g(x1,x2)(2x1,2+8x2)=-𝜆(2x1,2x2) We get the following equations
2x1=-𝜆(2x1)
2+8x2=-𝜆(2x2)
2x1=-𝜆(2x1)2+8x2=-𝜆(2x2) For (1)(1) to be satisfied, either 𝜆=-1𝜆=-1 or x1=0x1=0. CASE 1 :: -𝜆=1-𝜆=1Substitute -𝜆=1-𝜆=1 in (2)(2)
2+8x2=2x2
6x2=-2
x2=(-1/3)
2+8x2=2x26x2=-2x2=(-1/3) By primal feasibility, g(x1,x2)=0g(x1,x2)=0. Substitute x2=(-1/3)x2=(-1/3) in g(x1,x2)=0g(x1,x2)=0
x21+(-1/3)2-1=0
x21=(8/9)
x1=±8/9
x12+(-1/3)2-1=0x12=(8/9)x1=±8/9 CASE 2 :: x1=0x1=0Substitute x1=0x1=0 in g(x1,x2)=0g(x1,x2)=0

x2=±1

12. Projected Gradient DescentIt might be difficult to solve constrained optimization analytically, using the method of Lagrange multipliers. Here is an iterative algorithm. GOALminx [f(x)] s.t g(x)⩽0minx[f(x)]s.tg(x)⩽0Let 𝜂t>0𝜂t>0 STEP 1 :: Initialize x0x0STEP 2 :: For tt in {0,1,2,...,T}{0,1,2,...,T} xt+1=∏axt-𝜂▽f(xt)axt+1=∏(xt-𝜂▽f(xt))STEP 3 :: End • Gradient Step : xt-𝜂▽f(xt)xt-𝜂▽f(xt)• Projection Step : ∏ axt-𝜂▽f(xt)a∏(xt-𝜂▽f(xt)) Here, ∏ axa=
argmin
y∈S‖x-y‖2∏(x)=argminy∈S‖x-y‖2whereS=ayag(y)⩽0aS={y|g(y)⩽0}
CONSTRAINT SETS=ayag(y)⩽0aS={y|g(y)⩽0}xtxtxt-𝜂▽f(xt)xt-𝜂▽f(xt)
∏axt-𝜂▽f(xt)a
∏(xt-𝜂▽f(xt)) In the above picture, the projection step finds the point set SS, that is closest to xt-𝜂▽f(xt)xt-𝜂▽f(xt). If the constraint set is convex, then projected gradient descent converges to an optimal solution, under suitable conditions on the objective function.
13. Stochastic Gradient DescentLet us revisit linear regression (
week
-4)(week-4). Consider the following dataset.D={(x1,y1),...,(xn,yn}}D={(x1,y1),...,(xn,yn}}where, xi∈Rdxi∈Rd, and y∈Ry∈R, ∀i∈{1,...,n}∀i∈{1,...,n} • xi→xi→data point• yi→yi→corresponding label Consider matrix XX and YYX=a
―x1―1
―x2―1
⋮ ⋮
―xn―1
n×(d+1), Y=a
y1
y2
⋮
yn
n×1X=[―x1―1―x2―1⋮⋮―xn―1]n×(d+1),Y=[y1y2⋮yn]n×1 Every row of XX is a data point. All the entries in the last column of XX are 11. The goal is to find w∈Rd+1w^∈Rd+1 such thatw=
argmin
waaXw-Yaa2w^=argminw||Xw-Y||2 Let f(w)=aaXw-Yaa2f(w)=||Xw-Y||2. f(w)f(w) can also be written as follows::f(w)=n∑i=1(wTxi-yi)2f(w)=∑i=1n(wTxi-yi)2 Let us focus on hi(w)=(wTxi-yi)2hi(w)=(wTxi-yi)2. hi(w)hi(w) can be written as follows::hi(w)=p(qi(w))hi(w)=p(qi(w))where,a
p(x)=x2
qi(w)=wTxi+yi
p(x)=x2qi(w)=wTxi+yi qi(w)qi(w) is a linear function, and p(x)p(x) is a convex function. The compositon of a convex function and a linear function is convex. Therefore, hi(w)hi(w) is convex. f(w)f(w) is a sum of convex functions, and is therefore, convex.• Every local minima of ff is also a global minima• ww^ is a global minima if and only if ∇f(w)=0∇f(w^)=0 w=
argmin
wn∑i=1(wTxi-yi)2w^=argminw∑i=1n(wTxi-yi)2 The gradient of ff is given as follows::∇f(w)=XTXw-XTY∇f(w)=XTXw-XTY Setting ∇f(w)=0∇f(w)=0a
XTXw-XTY=0
w=(XTX)+XTY
XTXw-XTY=0w=(XTX)+XTY ++ symbol denotes the psuedoinverse. If XTXXTX has an inverse, thena
w=(XTX)-1XTY
w=(XTX)-1XTY The time complexity of computing (XTX)-1(XTX)-1 is O(d3)O(d3). If dd is large, then computing (XTX)-1(XTX)-1 would be computationally costly. For the objective function, every local minimum is also a global minimum. We know that the gradient descent algorithm converges to a local minimum. If dd is large, instead of computing (XTX)-1(XTX)-1, we can use gradient descent find ww^. Here is the algorithm. Let 𝜂t=1
t+1𝜂t=1t+1 • Initialize w0w0• for t∈{0,...,T}t∈{0,...,T}○ wt+1=wt-𝜂t(XTXwt-XTY)wt+1=wt-𝜂t(XTXwt-XTY)• End To compute the gradient, we need not find the inverse of XTXXTX. XTXT is a d×nd×n matrix and XX is an n×dn×d matrix. Suppose, nn, which corresponds to the number of data points is large, then computing XTXXTX would be costly. In such cases, we can make use of an algorithm called the stochastic gradient descent. Here is the algorithm. Let 𝜂t=1
t+1𝜂t=1t+1 • Initialize w0w0• For t∈{0,...,T-1}t∈{0,...,T-1}○ Sample kk data points (
where
 k≪n)(wherek≪n), and their corresponding lables from {(x1,y1),...,(xn,yn}){(x1,y1),...,(xn,yn}).○ Let the sampled data points be p1,...,pkp1,...,pk and let their corrsponding lables be q1,...,qkq1,...,qk○ Consider the matrices PP and QQP=a

―p2―1

―pk―1

qk
k×1P=[―p1―1―p2―1⋮⋮―pk―1]k×(d+1),Q=[q1q2⋮qk]k×1○ Update as followswt+1=wt-𝜂t(PTPwt-PTQ)wt+1=wt-𝜂t(PTPwt-PTQ)• End It can be shown that the average of1
T+1(T∑t=0wt)→w1T+1(∑t=0Twt)→w^
